coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework nanoprobe for direct and sensitive butyrylcholinesterase assay with non-enzymatic reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Kelin
Guo, Qi
Wu, Yiman
Lai, Yue
Yu, Ziyan
Liao, Yaxiao
Zou, Zhirong
Huang, Ke
coredata.dc:description: Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) was clinically considered an important biomarker for the diagnosis of liver diseases. Ellman's strategy (enzyme-catalyzed reaction) is normally utilized as a standard protocol for BChE activity sensing due to the different properties of substrate choline (Ch) and product thiocholine (TCh). However, there remain respective drawbacks besides the laborious operation procedure and time-consuming. We herein report the first example of using nanoscale zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8), self-assembled from 2-Methylimidazole (2-MIm) and Zn2+, to directly and target detect BChE. Encapsulation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) into ZIF-8 suppresses the emission of CDs, while the competitive coordination between BChE and the metal node of ZIF-8 can result in the release of CDs for BChE sensing. The sensing mechanism was studied in depth via experimental investigations, and multifarious luminescent guests, metal nodes, and organic ligands were employed to confirm that the rupture of ZIF-8 was due to the connection of BChE and Zn2+ caused by electrostatic attraction. The limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1.5 U L−1 was achieved with this sensing platform. Finally, this platform was applied to quantify BChE in 40 clinical serum samples of patients with clinical liver disease and showed great consistency with clinical diagnostic results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Butyrylcholinesterase
CDs@ZIF-8
Fluorescence
Non-enzymatic reaction
Clinical samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of BChE bioassay and possible mechanism
Application in real clinical human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of freeze-dried CDs power. (B) TEM image of CDs. (C) XRD spectra and (D) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8. SEM images of (E) ZIF-8 and (F) CDs@ZIF-8 (inset in: the corresponding TEM images).

. (A) Fluorescence peaks of CDs, CDs@ZIF-8, and ZIF-8 (inset in: fluorescence peaks of CDs@ZIF-8 incubated with 0, 20, 50, and 100 U L−1 BChE). (B) F/F0 of ZIF-8, CDs, and CDs@ZIF-8 reacted with BChE, respectively. (C) XRD pattern and (D) concentration of Zn2+ before and after the incubation. (E) The complete structure of CDs@ZIF-8; (F) Crushed surface of CDs@ZIF-8 after incubating with BChE.

Fluorescence peaks of (A) CDs@ZIF-8, (B) CdTe QDs@ZIF-8, (C) RhB@ZIF-8, (D) CDs@ZIF-67, (E) CDs@ZIF-90, (F) CDs@MOF-5 incubated with BChE. F/F0 signals comparisons of (G) different luminescent guests and (H) different metal nodes, and organic ligands. (I) Zeta potentials of the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-67, CDs@ZIF-90 and CDs@MOF-5.

. (A) F/F0 versus the concentration of BChE (inset in: the linear calibration curve between F/F0 and BChE in 5.0–160.0 U L−1 range). (B) Specificity of the sensor for BChE assay. Concentration: 20 µg mL−1 for BChE, 200 µg mL−1 for interferences.
The BChE values obtained by clinical and present method.
Principle of the encased CDs in ZIF-8 for BChE assay.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework nanoprobe for direct and sensitive butyrylcholinesterase assay with non-enzymatic reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610068, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Land Resources Evaluation and Monitoring in Southwest, Ministry of Education, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Land Resources Evaluation and Monitoring in Southwest, Ministry of Education, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Land Resources Evaluation and Monitoring in Southwest, Ministry of Education, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610068, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) was clinically considered an important biomarker for the diagnosis of liver diseases. Ellman's strategy (enzyme-catalyzed reaction) is normally utilized as a standard protocol for BChE activity sensing due to the different properties of substrate choline (Ch) and product thiocholine (TCh). However, there remain respective drawbacks besides the laborious operation procedure and time-consuming. We herein report the first example of using nanoscale zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8), self-assembled from 2-Methylimidazole (2-MIm) and Zn2+, to directly and target detect BChE. Encapsulation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) into ZIF-8 suppresses the emission of CDs, while the competitive coordination between BChE and the metal node of ZIF-8 can result in the release of CDs for BChE sensing. The sensing mechanism was studied in depth via experimental investigations, and multifarious luminescent guests, metal nodes, and organic ligands were employed to confirm that the rupture of ZIF-8 was due to the connection of BChE and Zn2+ caused by electrostatic attraction. The limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1.5 U L−1 was achieved with this sensing platform. Finally, this platform was applied to quantify BChE in 40 clinical serum samples of patients with clinical liver disease and showed great consistency with clinical diagnostic results.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of the encased CDs in ZIF-8 for BChE assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholinesterases (ChEs), mainly including acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), are essential enzymes in the human central nervous system [1]. BChE, different from AChE, is a nonspecific hydrolase in plasma that can hydrolyze choline (Ch) and some non-choline esters (e.g., cocaine, atropine, and procaine). Obvious reports have established that the underserved expression of BChE is closely interrelated to various diseases containing Parkinson’s disease, liver function damage, and Alzheimer’s disease [2]. In addition, BChE activity can be immensely restrained while the human body is invaded by organophosphates (Ops) [3]. Therefore, BChE has been regarded as a crucial biomarker for clinical diagnosis [4,5]. It is essential to develop reliable techniques for rapid and sensitive BChE activity assay.

For decades, various BChE detection methods have been invented including colorimetric [6], fluorescence [7,8], radiometric [9], and electrochemical assay [10]. Ellman's strategy (enzyme-catalyzed reaction) is normally utilized as a standard protocol for BChE activity determination: BChE can hydrolyze choline (ATCh or BTCh) into thiocholine (TCh) [6]. The sulfhydryl group of TCh can combine with (reduction or complexation) nanomaterials or fluorescent probes to change their color or fluorescence or electrochemical intensity. For example, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be oxidized to ox-TMB with blueness in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets (NSs), while the transformation was restrained based on the interaction between TCh and MnO2 [11]. Furthermore, Iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH), employed as a fluorescence quencher for gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), could be resolved by enzymatic hydrolysate (TCh) and thus inducing the recovery of the quenched fluorescence intensity (FI) [12]. Moreover, an electrochemical sensor took advantage of carbon black and Prussian blue on paper was developed to detect TCh and hence indirectly measured the activity of BChE [13]. In our previous works, a fluorescent and visual method for the determination of BChE and its inhibitor was developed based on modulating the synthesis of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) by an enzymatic reaction [14]. Afterwards, we also developed a dual-mode method for BChE activity monitoring based on the disaggregation of MnO2 NSs via inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and smartphone color recognizer [15]. Although these indirect detection methods have satisfying precision, favorable linear range, and sensitivity, laborious operation procedures and severe conditions are also needed. And it often takes a long time in the hydrolytic process. Some well-designed targeting probes with high specificity have been designed based on the direct and precise recognition of ChEs. Yang’s group constructed a BChE-specific probe (BChE-FP) based on the conspicuous fluorescence escalation of the probe in the existence of BChE [16]. Moreover, Han’s group devised a particular fluorescent probe containing a fluorescent group with specific appetency to albumin [17]. Though these constructed probes show high sensitivity and strong fluorescence, the preparation procedure containing specific groups is often complex and fussy.

Metal organic framework (MOF), assembled by organic groups and transition metal ions, is known as a new type of porous material. According to the diverse structural, customizable pore sizes, high surface area, and various functionalities, MOF has been widely used in catalysis, gas adsorption, biosensing etc [18]. Recently, MOF has been effectively utilized as a luminous guest host for the fabrication of nanoprobes. Significantly improved analytical performances can be realized in such MOF-based nanoprobes by releasing fluorescent species such as fluorescein, dyes, QDs, and nanoclusters [19,20]. And satisfactory results have been obtained for metal ions, anions, and biomarkers sensing via peculiar interaction between MOF and target analytes [21]. In the presented study, ZIF-8 was chosen as a typical MOF for BChE biosensing. ZIF-8 was synthesized by assembling Zn2+ with organic imidazolyl ligands in appropriate solvents. Zn is an essential element in the human immune system, and imidazolyl is part of the amino acid histidine [22]. Due to its excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, ZIF-8 was regarded as one of the most suitable MOFs for biosensing in tumor microenvironment and intracellular environment. For example, a fluorescence recovery strategy has been successfully applied to adenosine triphosphate (ATP) sensing by releasing fluorescent Rhodamine B (RhB) from the Rhodamine B/zeolitic imidazole framework (RhB/ZIF-90) [23]. And a doxycycline (DC) assay method was developed based on the DC can bind to Zn2+ in ZIF-8, thus inducing the disassembly of ZIF-8 and triggering the instinct fluorescence of DC [24]. In this work, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were enclosed in the ZIF-8. The CDs have the advantages of unique fluorescent properties, cost-effective synthesis, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [25]. The integration of CDs and ZIF takes the best of both approaches, including excellent optical properties (such as tunable emission lights and photochemical stability) and high surface areas, tunable porosity, and designable functionalities, which significantly improved their sensitivity and selectivity for BChE assay [21].

It has been reported that histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), and several other amino acids in the enzyme or protein have affinity to metal ions (Zn2+, for instance) [26]. In our current work, a novel and speedy sensor was assembled for direct sensing BChE by the CDs encased zeolitic imidazolate framework (CDs@ZIF-8). Encapsulation of fluorescent CDs into ZIF-8 can restrain the fluorescence-emission of CDs, while the competitive combination between BChE and the metal node (Zn2+) of ZIF-8 via electrostatic attraction, resulting in the release of the fluorescence of CDs for BChE sensing through fluorescence enhancing ( Scheme 1). X-ray diffraction (XRD), ICP-MS, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), and scanning electron microscope (SEM) were utilized to characterize the transformations of CDs@ZIF-8 before and after the incubation with BChE. The mechanism shows that the anion site of the BChE active center can attract the positive ZIF-8 due to electrostatic interaction, thus bridging the distance between BChE and ZIF-8, making it possible for capable amino acids in BChE to bind with Zn2+ in ZIF-8, and also laying the foundation for the direct sensing of BChE. Therefore, the feasible platform for BChE assay by monitoring the variation of the systemic fluorescence was established. 40 clinical serum samples were collected and analyzed to evaluate the accuracy of this proposed strategy. The testing results were consistent with clinical diagnostic detection, denoting that liver disease patients could be distinguished from normal people. As a typical approach, this method allowed for the simple, direct and sensitive evaluation of the BChE activity with easy sample handling and friendly experimental conditions.

All the reagents and apparatus used in this work are detailed in the Supporting information (SI). The synthesis procedure of CDs@ZIF-67, CDs@ZIF-90, CDs@MOF-5, CdTe QDs@ZIF-8, and RhB@ZIF-8 are also shown in SI.

CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment according to the previous work [27]. Typically, citric acid (0.46 g) and o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, 0.21 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixed solution was packed in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 5 h heating at 200 ºC. The obtained CDs solution was then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove large particles. Subsequently, the products were subjected to dialysis by a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove impurities or unreacted substances and then freeze dried to light yellow powder. The final CDs solution was obtained by dissolving the CDs powder into ultrapure water (1.0 mg mL−1) and stored at 4 ºC.

Our homemade CDs@ZIF-8 was fabricated following the reported method with little modifications [24]. 2 mL 2-Methylimidazole (2-MIm) in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solution (0.4 mol L−1) followed mixed as-prepared CDs solution (200 µL). Then a DMF solution of Zn(CH3COOH)2·2 H2O (2 mL, 0.2 mol L−1) was rapidly poured into the mixture. After stirring for 5 min, 5 mL of DMF was mixed to further stabilize the resulting generates. The resultant CDs@ZIF-8 was separated by centrifugal separation, and dried overnight at 65 ºC after being washed with DMF (once) and ethanol. ZIF-8 materials were synthesized based on the steps described above without the addition of CDs solution.

40 serum samples were centrifuged using 100 kDa ultrafiltration tubes (8000 rpm, 15 min) to remove interferences (such as glutathione (GSH) and human serum albumin (HSA)), and then diluted 100 times and stored at 4 ºC.

CDs@ZIF-8 power was dispersed in water (0.1 mg mL−1) before usage. Briefly, 500 µL of CDs@ZIF-8 solution was added to 500 µL of different concentrations of BChE. Ultimately, fluorescence was subjected after incubating the reaction solution for 15 min at 37 ºC (excited at 375 nm).

The prepared CDs and ZIF-8 were characterized first. As presented in Fig. 1A, there was a remarkable broad peak at around 20º in the XRD pattern of the prepared CDs, showing the highly disordered carbon atoms in the CDs [25]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image presents the uniform spherical shape and great dispersibility of CDs (Fig. 1B). The mean diameter of the CDs was found to be 3.24 nm (Fig. S1). To investigate the optical properties of CDs in the water, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. The optimal fluorescence emission was at 437 nm when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. In addition, the maximum peak at 370 nm obtained by UV–vis absorption spectrum was attributed to n-π * switching of CN and CO (Fig. S2) [25]. The fluorescence spectrum shows excitation-independent property of CDs in the range of 345–390 nm (Fig. S3), which may be originated by the uniform dispersity and surface states of CDs [28].

As illustrated in XRD pattern (Fig. 1C), there was a series of obvious diffraction peaks of 2θ at 7.2, 10.3, 12.6, 14.5, 16.3 and 17.9º in ZIF-8, which were consistent with the stimulated one [29]. Similar diffraction peaks were obtained by CDs@ZIF-8, confirming that the crystalline structure of ZIF-8 was constant after the addition of CDs. The same phenomenon was also observed in Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra. The characteristic absorption peaks in FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 were consistent with previous works (Fig. 1D) [30]. The peaks in the range of 700–1500 cm−1 represented the bending and stretching vibration of the imidazole ring. The peak centered at 1586 cm−1 corresponded to the CN stretching mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 were shown in Fig. S4. The peaks at 1021.3 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1044.3 eV (Zn 2p1/2) demonstrated a spin-energy separation of 23.0 eV, which was related to Zn (II) in ZIF-8 (Fig. S4A) [29]. As shown in Fig. S4B, three peaks appeared at 283.8, 284.6, and 285.7 eV corresponded to sp2 carbon, sp3 carbon, and C-N bond, respectively [29,31]. The binding energies of C-N bond for N 1s were measured at 398.2 eV (ZIF-8) and 398.3 eV (CDs@ZIF-8) with a slight energy shift of 0.1 eV (Fig. S4C). The peaks at 531.0 and 532.4 eV in O 1s region were deconvoluted to Zn-OH group and H2O (Fig. S4D), respectively [32]. All these results indicated that chemical composition and valence state of the ZIF-8 were kept well after the addition of CDs. SEM was utilized to evaluate the morphological difference of the prepared nano-probe. As illustrated in Fig. 1E and F, ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 both emerged as identical polyhedral shape, and the diameter of crystals was around 200 nm and 250 nm for ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, respectively. The rhombic dodecahedron morphology of CDs@ZIF-8 was clearly observed by the atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. S5A). AFM 3D height images (Fig. S5B and S5C) showed the representative nanocrystal with height of ∼200 nm and base width of ∼260 nm, which were in accordance with the SEM images. To confirm the integration of CDs and ZIF-8, TEM characterization was presented (inset in Fig. 1E and F). The TEM image of CDs@ZIF-8 (inset in Fig. 1F) shows that CDs can be observed inside the structure of ZIF-8. To further demonstrate the integration of CDs and ZIF-8, the contrast of fluorescent images by adding CDs before and after ZIF-8 synthesis was provided under 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. S6, strong fluorescence can be observed in both CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S6A) and CDs and ZIF-8 mixture (Fig. S6B). As anticipated, the fluorescence of the supernatant of CDs@ZIF-8 after centrifugation and washing was weak and transparent, whereas the supernatant of CDs and ZIF-8 mixture solution had significant fluorescence intensity, revealing that CDs were firmly loaded into ZIF-8 to form CDs@ZIF-8. All above results support that ZIF-8 can retain its internal composition and external appearance after adding CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of BChE bioassay and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As reflected in Fig. 2A, the pure CDs solution exhibited strong FI while the pure ZIF-8 has bare FI. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the system was significantly decreased by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-8 in this platform. After incubation with incremental concentration (0–100 U L−1) of BChE, there were a series of elevated fluorescent peaks centered around 436 nm (inset in Fig. 2A). Meanwhile, when BChE was reacted with CDs or ZIF-8, there was no obvious change of F/F0 (F and F0 severally corresponded to the FI of the reaction solution existence and non-existence BChE) (Fig. 2B). These attempts indicate the sensing system for BChE detection based on MOF encapsulated CDs was possible.

To explore the potential sensing mechanism, characterizations such as XRD, ICP-MS, SEM, and NMR were applied. After incubation CDs@ZIF-8 with BChE, feeble diffraction peaks of XRD spectra from the power filtrated from the mixed solution was obtained, indicating the minimal crystal content and broken crystal texture of CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2C). The concentration of dissociated Zn2+ in filtrates before and after incubating BChE were measured by ICP-MS. The results reveal that the content of Zn2+ was significantly increased after reacting with BChE, indicating the ZIF was broken (which cannot be intercepted on the 0.22 µm filter membrane) (Fig. 2D). On the other hand, the appearance of CDs@ZIF-8 dispersed in water was uniform and complete before reaction (Fig. 2E). As shown in Fig. 2F, after mixing with BChE, the ZIF-8 shell was broken, which confirmed the exact reaction between BChE and ZIF-8. In addition, chemical shifts of the materials were measured by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S7). The results shows that Δδ of H-a (0.03 ppm) and H-b (0.04 ppm) may be attributed to the change of the chemical environment caused by the variations in the coordination bond between N and Zn2+. All above results demonstrate that the BChE assay strategy is based on the chemical reaction between ZIF-8 and BChE. After reaction, the encased CDs was released by decomposition of ZIF-8 shell, resulting the increase of F/F0 signals.

To investigate the essential of reaction between ZIF-8 and BChE, different luminescent guests (LG), metal nodes, and organic ligands were carried out and utilized to prepare composite materials. Firstly, multiple LG including CdTe QDs and RhB were separately packaged in ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 3A-C, all FI of CDs@ZIF-8, CdTe QDs@ZIF-8 and RhB@ZIF-8 increased continuously after incubating with BChE. When the concentrations of BChE were increased from 0 U to 100 U L−1, the F/F0 of different materials increased in a similar trend, which means no substantive effect played by LG embedded in MOFs (Fig. 3G). Furthermore, ZIF-67 (coordination between 2-MIm and Co2+), ZIF-90 (coordination between Imidazole-2-carboxaldehyde (2-ICA) and Zn2+) and MOF-5 (coordination between terephthalic acid (TPA) and Zn2+) were also used as CDs containing shell to investigate the influence of the metal nodes, and organic ligands. The results showed that the increased FI could be observed by CDs@ZIF-67 (Fig. 3D), while fluorescence peaks of CDs@ZIF-90 presented a slight upward trend (less than 5 %) (Fig. 3E). And there was no obvious variation exhibited in CDs@MOF-5 (Fig. 3F). F/F0 values of above CDs@MOFs are also shown in Fig. 3H. Combined with the zeta potentials (Fig. 3I), it can be seen that the distance could be decreased by the electrostatic attraction between MOF with positive surface potential (such as CDs@ZIF-8 (19.90 mV) and CDs@ZIF-67 (19.93 mV)) and the anionic active site of BChE, thus making the coordination of the amino acids of BChE with metal ions (e.g., Zn2+, Co2+) possible. In particular, CDs@ZIF-90 (−4.00 mV) and CDs@MOF-5 (−9.84 mV) with negative surface potentials exhibited that there were no obvious increase after adding BChE. These results kept consistent with the previous report that the anionic center of ChEs could attract with the sites carried positive charge, thus decreasing the distance to improve the reaction efficiency [33]. To further explore the mechanism, parathion-methyl (PM) was used as the BChE inhibitor. After the inhibition of BChE by PM, the F/F0 signals gradually reduced, (Fig. S8), indicating that the reaction of BChE and ZIF-8 is attenuated when the anionic site and the serine residue of BChE are specifically bounded by PM. All of these results show that the active centers of BChE are the key to trigger the rupture of ZIF-8.

The optimal conditions were presented in Fig. S9-S11. In this case, FI (Fig. S12) and F/F0 ( Fig. 4 A) of the reaction solution is gradually enhanced with the BChE activity increase from 3.0 to 200.0 U L−1, respectively. The calibration curve (F/F0 = 0.003 [BChE] + 1.0198) is linear in 5.0–160.0 U L−1 range with a good linear relationship (R
2 = 0.9972) and the limit of detection (LOD) of BChE is 1.5 U L−1 (3 σ/S) (inset in Fig. 4A). And the limit of quantitation (LOQ) of this system is calculated to be 4.9 U L−1 The superior precision and repeatability for the BChE detection can be evaluated by a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 7) of 1.6 %. Comparing with previously reported techniques (Table S1), the proposed method displays comparable performance, but it is more simple without long time hydrolytic process.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensing platform, various species including metal ions (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, and Ca2+), amino acids (Lysine, Arginine, and Cysteine), proteases (cellulase, pepsin, and trypsin), and biomolecules (GSH and ascorbic acid (AA)) intrinsic components in the serum were selected as interferences. As disclosed in Fig. 4B, there was no obvious enhance of F/F0 for the majority of species except GSH, Cys, and AA. The interference of GSH and Cys could be effectively eliminated by mixing special thiols scavenger N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), which was reported in the previous works [15]. As shown in Fig. 4B, no obvious interference was observed after incubation of GSH or Cys with NEM (0.3 M), respectively. The normal level of AA in the serum was 22.71–85.17 μM. In this work, the sample solution was diluted 100 times before detection due to the high concentration of BChE in the serum (5100–12600 U L−1 in normal human serum). Therefore, we studied the influence of AA by adding 1 µg mL−1 (approximately 5.67 μM) AA in the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 4B, the results show that 1 µg mL−1 AA can not cause obvious interference for BChE assay. Clearly, the developed platform performs superior sensitivity and selectivity for BChE detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in real clinical human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the different parts of the presence of AChE and BChE in the human body, serum samples were selected to recede the effect of AChE. 3 serum samples (No. 15, 32, and 35 in Fig. 5) of patients with liver disease (obtained from West China Hospital of Sichuan University) were selected to verify the accuracy of the presented method. As shown in Table S2, spiked recovery validation was utilized according to the guidelines of bioanalytical method validation. The results show that the spiked recoveries are in the range of 95–105 %. The t-test was used to validate the significant differences between the added value and determined value of BChE, with no significant differences found at the 90 % confidence level. The regular BChE level is 5100–12600 U L−1 in a healthy people's serum [34]. A reduced BChE activity in serum can direct contact with liver parenchyma injuries [35]. To investigate the practicability of our proposed BChE sensing method in real clinical application, 40 human serum samples from healthy volunteers and patients with liver disease were performed after diluting 100-fold. Fig. 5 shows the detected values by this method were in accordance with the clinical results measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). What’s more, a wide range of BChE activities (in a wide range of 1500–9000 U L−1) can be accurate assay in this sensing platform (Table S3). The specially-marked samples with low BChE activity in Fig. 5 were confirmed to be positive for liver disease. Overall, these results point out the developed sensor is appropriate for BChE sensing in clinical tests.

In the present work, by encapsulating fluorescent CDs on the ZIF-8, we successfully developed an off-on fluorescence sensor for sensing BChE in clinical human serum. The sensing system can be rapidly prepared in a short time at room temperature and carried out without enzymatic reaction. The CDs@ZIF-8 platform shows a wide ranging from 5 to 160 U L−1 with a detection LOD as low as 1.5 U L−1. The developed method without fussy sample preparation can realize rapid detection for BChE under low-hanging experimental conditions. The mechanism was carefully studied to confirm that the rupture of ZIF-8 was caused by connection of BChE and Zn2+ due to the electrostatic attraction. On this basis, our work may cast a new light on the direct assay for BChE without any enzyme substrates. Investigating a more precise reaction between the core-shell nanocomposites and BChE might prove important in future work.

Kelin Hu: performed the experiments and wrote the manuscript, Zhirong Zou, Ke Huang: designed and guided the experiments, and revised the manuscript, Qi Guo, Yiman Wu: performed the synthesis and characterization of ZIF and CDs and data processing, Yue Lai, Ziyuan Yu: performed clinical experiments, Yaxiao Liao: revised the original draft of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605108 and 22106113), and Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133825.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observation of the elevation of cholinesterase activity in brain glioma by a near-infrared emission chemsensor
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, W. Gao, S. Ma, Y. Liu, W. Lin, Observation of the elevation of cholinesterase activity in brain glioma by a near-infrared emission chemsensor, Anal. Chem., 92 (2020) 13405–13410.

Sens. Actuators B-Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kelin Hu is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward element speciation.
Qi Guo is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward biosensors.
Yiman Wu is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward biosensors.
Yue Lai is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward fluorescence sensors.
Ziyan Yu is an undergraduate student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward fluorescence sensors.
Yaxiao Liao is an undergraduate student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward fluorescence sensors.
Zhirong Zou is a lecturer in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. He is currently working toward the colorimetric sensing.

Ke Huang received his B.Sc. degree in 2010 from the College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Ph.D. degree in 2015 in analytical chemistry from the College of Chemistry, Sichuan University. He began his own independent career in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. His current research interest is analytical spectroscopy.
